🍕 The "Dummy-Proof" Guide to Circles

Visual, interactive, and completely broken down for test day.

10.1 Basics 10.2 Angles & Arcs 10.3 Chords 10.4 Inscribed 10.5 Tangents 10.6 Angle Math 10.7 Segments 10.8 Equations

10.1 - Basics & Circumference

A circle is just a bunch of points perfectly spaced out from a center dot.

🧠 Dummy Translation: Think of a pizza. The radius is a slice from the center to the crust. The diameter is a cut straight across the whole pizza. Circumference is the length of the outer crust.
Circumference (C) = 2 × π × r   OR   C = π × d

Visualizer: Radius & Diameter

50

Diameter: 100 | Circumference: 314.16

10.2 - Central Angles & Arcs

A Central Angle is an angle whose vertex (pointy part) sits exactly dead-center in the circle.

An Arc is just a piece of the outside crust. Minor arcs are small (<180°). Major arcs are massive (>180°).

🧠 Dummy Translation: The size of your pizza slice (Central Angle) is EXACTLY EQUAL to the degree size of your crust (Arc Measure).

Visualizer: The Pizza Slice Match

Central Angle = 70° SO Intercepted Arc = 70°

10.3 - Arcs and Chords

A Chord is a straight line connecting two points on the crust. The 90-Degree Rule: If a radius hits a chord at a perfect 90° angle, it chops that chord perfectly in half!

🛠️ How to Solve "Missing Chord" Problems:

  1. Draw a line from the center to the edge of the chord. Congratulations, you just drew a Radius! You now have a Right Triangle.
  2. Identify your pieces: The radius is the hypotenuse (c). The distance from the center is a leg (a). Half the chord is the other leg (b).
  3. Use Pythagorean Theorem: a² + b² = c² to find the missing side.
  4. Multiply by 2 if they ask for the full chord length!

Visualizer: The Hidden Right Triangle

Move the slider to push the chord away from the center. Watch the Right Triangle form!

Radius (c) = 100
a² + b² = c² → 60² + b² = 100² → Half-Chord (b) = 80
Full Chord Length = 160

10.4 - Inscribed Angles

An Inscribed Angle has its vertex pushed all the way back ON the crust (edge of the circle), not in the center.

🧠 Dummy Translation: Because the angle is pushed so far back, it is WEAK. It is exactly HALF the size of the crust (arc) it traps.

Visualizer: The "Half-Size" Rule

If Arc is 100°, then Inscribed Angle is 50°

10.5 - Tangents

A Tangent is a line that brushes past the circle, touching it at exactly ONE single point.

🧠 The 2 Golden Rules of Tangents:
1. Radius + Tangent line = ALWAYS a perfect 90° right angle. (Yes, more Pythagorean theorem!)
2. The Party Hat Rule: If two tangent lines come from the exact same point outside the circle, they are equal in length.

Visualizer: The Party Hat Rule

Top Tangent = 100 | Bottom Tangent = 100

10.6 - Angles with Secants & Tangents

To find angle measures of crossing lines, you just have to look at WHERE the angle's corner (vertex) is.

Visualizer: The "Where is the Angle?" Game

10.7 - Segment Length Math

Now we are doing math with the actual lengths of the sticks (segments) crossing the circle, not angles!

🛠️ How to set up the math:

If crossing INSIDE (The X):
Multiply the two pieces of the same stick.
Piece 1 × Piece 2 = Piece 3 × Piece 4

If crossing OUTSIDE (The V):
Multiply the outside piece by the ENTIRE stick.
Outside × WHOLE = Outside × WHOLE

⚠️ "WHOLE" means you must ADD the outside + inside parts together!

Visualizer: Segment Length Formulas

10.8 - Equations of Circles

Circles on a coordinate graph have their own special equation based on the Pythagorean theorem.

(x - h)² + (y - k)² = r²
🧠 Dummy Translation:
1. Center = (h, k). THE SIGNS FLIP! If the formula says (x - 3)², the center x is POSITIVE 3.
2. Radius = r. The number at the end is the radius SQUARED. You must take the square root to find the actual radius length.

Visualizer: Circle Grapher



2


-1


3

(x - 2)² + (y + 1)² = 9
